figures.gloss: The pre-eternal, absolute Divine Essence. Before the cosmos existed, Khavandagar dwelled silently within a primordial pearl. From this hidden state, the Essence brought forth creation by manifesting its light through successive emanations and avatars across the epochs.
figures.facet: Khavandagar is rarely approached directly. The Supreme Essence remains entirely unmanifest and unknowable except through its physical incarnations (the 'libas' or garments) and the primordial heptad of angels it emanated to govern the material world.
figures.provenance: Kalam-e Saranjam; 'Shahnameh-ye Haqiqat' of Hajj Ne'matollah.

figures.gloss: The great 14th-century reformer and the fourth absolute incarnation of the Divine Essence. He reorganized the Yarsani faith, revealed the ultimate esoteric truth (Haqiqa), and established the sacred rituals and lineages that govern the community today.
figures.facet: Unlike an Abrahamic prophet who merely receives words from God, Sultan Sahak is understood as the literal, bodily manifestation of God on earth for his epoch. He does not point to the Divine; he is the Divine wrapped in a human garment.
figures.provenance: Core Yarsani Kalams; oral traditions of the Hawraman region; Tadhkira-i A'la.

figures.gloss: The seven primordial archangels created from Khavandagar's pure light. They are the eternal architects and guardians of the cosmos, accompanying the Divine Avatar in every historical epoch by taking on human forms to guide the faithful.
figures.facet: They are not subordinate created beings with free will, but consubstantial rays of the Divine. Their roles in the community (such as the Pir and the Dalil) are absolute, mapping eternal cosmic functions directly onto living spiritual lineages.
figures.provenance: Kalam-e Saranjam; the Covenant of Perdiwar.

figures.gloss: The doctrine of the soul's transmigration. A soul must travel through a strict sequence of 1,001 'garments' (dun), or physical incarnations. Each life is a crucible dictating the soul's ascent toward purity or descent into base matter.
figures.facet: Unlike Eastern models of endless, infinite rebirth, Dunadun is a strictly closed system. The 1,001-incarnation limit provides a definitive terminus, merging the mechanics of reincarnation with a distinct eschatological timeline of final judgment and salvation.

figures.gloss: The central ritual gathering of Yarsanism. Initiated members sit in an unbroken circle, erasing all worldly hierarchies, to chant sacred Kalams, play the tanbur, and share consecrated offerings (nazr or niyaz) of food.
figures.facet: The Jam is not merely symbolic; it is a literal collapsing of space and time. A properly convened Jam recreates the primordial divine council of Perdiwar on earth, achieving direct, unmediated communion (Haqiqa) with the Divine Essence.
figures.provenance: Ritual prescriptions established by Sultan Sahak at Perdiwar.

figures.gloss: The first and greatest of the Haft Tan, representing the eternal condition of the Pir (spiritual master). He is the indispensable guide and intercessor for all 'Ahl-e Haqq' (People of Truth), tasked with initiating souls into the divine mystery.
figures.facet: Salvation is impossible without pledging oneself to a Pir. Pir Benjamin is the cosmic archetype of this absolute necessity, demonstrating that spiritual liberation requires strict initiation and submission to a living, incarnate hierarchy.
figures.provenance: The Covenant of Perdiwar; Shart-o-Iqrar (The Pact).

figures.gloss: The supreme holy text of Yarsanism, comprising the divine utterances revealed to Sultan Sahak and his companions. It contains the ultimate spiritual laws, cosmological secrets, and historical trajectory of the faith.
figures.facet: It is a fiercely protected, esoteric corpus. Unlike universalist scriptures meant for global conversion, the Kalams are intentionally hidden, transmitted largely orally among the initiated, prioritizing lived spiritual descent over public textualism.
figures.provenance: Manuscripts preserved by the Guran sayyids; oral recitation traditions.

figures.gloss: The sacred village and bridge over the Sirwan River where Sultan Sahak established the ultimate covenant of Yarsanism. It is the spiritual epicenter of the tradition, the site where the era of esoteric Truth (Haqiqa) formally began.
figures.facet: Perdiwar anchors the divine entirely in local geography. The metaphysical bridge of judgment is brought down to the physical mountains of Kurdistan, permanently sealing the cosmic pact between Khavandagar and humanity in a specific, tangible place.
figures.provenance: Hagiography of Sultan Sahak; geography of the Hawraman region.

figures.gloss: In the Yarsani cycle of divine garments (dun), Ali is held to be the second manifestation of the Divine Essence, appearing in the epoch after Khavandagar's primordial garment and before Sultan Sahak's. He is not read as the historical Sunni/Shi'a caliph but as a fully transfigured theophany.
figures.facet: Outsiders reduce the whole community to 'Ali-Allahi,' but within the tradition Ali is only one station in a longer avatar sequence — the label mistakes a single garment in the dun cycle for the whole of the doctrine.
figures.provenance: V. Minorsky, 'Notes sur la secte des Ahl-i Haqq' and the EI2 'Ahl-i Hakk' entry; M.R. Hamzeh'ee, 'The Yaresan: A Sociological, Historical and Religio-Historical Study of a Kurdish Community.'

figures.gloss: A theophany of the Divine Essence held to have appeared in Lorestan in the epoch before Sultan Sahak, remembered in the kalam traditions of the Lak and Lur regions rather than the Kurdish Hawraman heartland.
figures.facet: Shows the avatar cycle is not a Hawraman-only sequence: Shah Khoshin roots an earlier station of the same dun doctrine among Lak- and Luri-speaking Yarsani communities to the south and east.
figures.provenance: Z. Mir-Hosseini, 'Redefining the Truth: Shi'ism and the Ahl-i Haqq'; Hamzeh'ee, 'The Yaresan.'

figures.gloss: One of the Haft Tan, holding the eternal office of Dalil (Guide). With Pir Benjamin he is among the most frequently invoked companions in Yarsani devotion, called upon in kalams and oaths as rescuer of the faithful in distress.
figures.facet: Dawud completes the paired offices of Pir and Dalil that structure every Yarsani's initiatory bond to the Haft Tan — master and guide together, not the Pir alone.
figures.provenance: Mir-Hosseini, 'Redefining the Truth'; Kalam-e Saranjam oral kalams.

figures.gloss: The sole female member of the Haft Tan and the virgin mother of Sultan Sahak — the one feminine person within the otherwise all-male heptad of archangels.
figures.facet: Her motherhood of the fourth avatar folds female sanctity into the avatar cycle without her becoming an avatar herself, distinguishing the heptad's structure from a simple gender-symmetric pairing.

figures.gloss: A revered companion-figure of the Sultan Sahak epoch, remembered as a poet and holy man. His mountain shrine near Sarpol-e Zahab in the Zagros foothills is among the most-visited living Yarsani pilgrimage sites.
figures.facet: Baba Yadgar shows the avatar cycle radiating a wider halo of lesser holy companions beyond the Haft Tan proper, whose shrines anchor lived devotion between the great jam gatherings.
figures.provenance: Hamzeh'ee, 'The Yaresan'; M. van Bruinessen's surveys of Yarsani/Ahl-e Haqq shrines.

figures.gloss: The sacred long-necked lute of Yarsanism, kissed before playing and reserved from secular use — the sole instrument through which the Kalams are voiced in the Jam.
figures.facet: The tanbur collapses medium and message: its maqam melodies are held to carry part of the revelation itself, not to accompany a separate spoken text.
figures.provenance: Kalam-e Saranjam ritual prescriptions; ethnomusicological accounts of Yarsani sacred maqam (e.g., work summarized in Hamzeh'ee, 'The Yaresan').

figures.gloss: The initiation binding every Yarsani to a Pir and a Dalil, sealed by breaking a nutmeg shared between novice and guide — the individual's re-enactment of the collective Covenant of Perdiwar.
figures.facet: Sar Sepordan turns Pir Benjamin's abstract doctrine of required submission into a concrete, once-in-a-lifetime rite: without it, membership in the Ahl-e Haqq is void.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The Divine Avatar and his court of seven eternal archangels, who always manifest together.
Intra-tradition: The founder and the sacred axis where he revealed the Truth and sealed the covenant.
Intra-tradition: The cycle of incarnation is navigated and purified through participation in the sacred assembly.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature a primordial heptad of divine emanations governing creation, reflecting a deeply documented historical continuum in Iranian religious thought.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme creator pre-existing in a sealed enclosure (the pearl/egg) before expanding outward to generate the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The utterly hidden, indivisible source of all existence from which all lower spiritual orders and emanations flow.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The infinite, unmanifest Godhead that cannot be known in itself, only through its successive emanations.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct bodily incarnation of the Divine Word, descending to act as the ultimate bridge between humanity and the absolute.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A complete, structured divine realm of emanated beings that mediate between the unknowable God and the material universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The eternal soul traveling through material forms to shed impurities and accrue consequences, though Yarsanism limits this process strictly to 1,001 lifetimes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism denies a persistent self across rebirths, Dunadun insists on a singular, accountable essence moving through different physical garments.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly structured ritual assembly that literally collapses time, allowing participants to recreate and participate in the primordial divine council.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unshakeable cosmic center where the divine intersects with the earthly, though Perdiwar is anchored to a literal river bridge rather than a mythological peak.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The geographical and metaphysical anchor of the universe, serving as the throne of the highest spiritual authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A strictly bounded, divinely revealed corpus that dictates the exact spiritual and ritual order, kept esoteric and closed to outsiders.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial archetype of the spiritual guide and revealer of esoteric knowledge, to whom all seekers must trace their initiation.
Intra-tradition: successive stations of the same Divine Essence in the dun cycle of theophanies.
Intra-tradition: earlier and later avatars in the same closed sequence of incarnations.
Intra-tradition: the Lorestan theophany preceding the fourth and definitive avatar.
Intra-tradition: the paired offices of Dalil (guide) and Pir (master) that structure Yarsani initiatory lineage.
Intra-tradition: the virgin mother and the avatar-son of the Haft Tan.
Intra-tradition: a beloved companion-figure of the Sultan Sahak epoch, honored at his own pilgrimage shrine.
Intra-tradition: the sacralized instrument through which the Kalams are voiced in the sacred assembly.
Intra-tradition: the individual rite that re-enacts the collective covenant sealed at Perdiwar.
Ahl-e Haqq and Alawite doctrine both deify Ali as a divine emanation, attested strands of the same historical ghulat (extremist Shi'a) continuum.
Ahl-e Haqq, Alevism and Alawism form a documented historical family of ghulat traditions that fuse Ali into the Godhead itself.
two closely related Kurdish esoteric traditions each anchor authority in a jealously guarded, largely oral scriptural corpus rather than a public written canon.

The ghulat floor beneath the ceiling: Twelver Imamah holds Ali a human, divinely-guided successor, while the Ahl-e Haqq avatar-cycle (already linked to Alawite and Alevi deification) makes him one incarnate body of the same Divine Essence.

dossier.essence: An esoteric religion of the western Iranian, largely Kurdish, world, concentrated in Kermanshah province and the Guran and Hawraman regions, whose adherents call themselves Ahl-e Haqq, 'People of Truth.' Reorganized in the 14th-15th centuries by Sultan Sahak at Perdiwar, it teaches that the one divine Essence manifests in a series of human incarnations and that salvation comes through initiation, the sacred assembly, and the soul's long journey through rebirth. Its teachings are transmitted esoterically, largely within hereditary sacred lineages.
dossier.cosmos: The absolute Essence, Khavandagar, dwelt hidden within a primordial pearl and unfolds creation through successive theophanies across the ages, accompanied each time by the Haft Tan, seven angelic beings of pure light who take human form to guide humanity; Sultan Sahak is understood as the definitive manifestation of the present epoch. Souls transmigrate through a bounded sequence of 'garments' (dunadun), up to 1,001 incarnations, as a graded purification ending in judgment and return to the divine light.
dossier.practice: The central rite is the Jam, a seated assembly of initiates in an unbroken circle that dissolves worldly rank, featuring recitation of sacred poems (kalam) to the sacred lute (tanbur) and the sharing of consecrated food-offerings (niyaz), understood to recreate the primordial covenant of Perdiwar. Every believer must be bound to a spiritual master (Pir); ethics stress purity, truth, and the sacred vow, and the community guards its teachings closely against outsiders.
dossier.canon: A largely oral and guarded corpus of sacred poems, much of it in Gorani, above all the Kalam-e Saranjam ('Discourse of Conclusion'); the later verse 'Shahnameh-ye Haqiqat' of Hajj Ne'matollah Jayhunabadi is an important account.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds religion as guarded, initiatory truth, the divine known through a chain of human incarnations and reached in a ritual circle that abolishes hierarchy and collapses sacred time.
sources: Vladimir Minorsky, 'Notes sur la secte des Ahle-Haqq' (1920)
Ziba Mir-Hosseini, 'Inner Truth and Outer History: The Two Worlds of the Ahl-i Haqq of Kurdistan' (1994)
Philip G. Kreyenbroek & Yiannis Kanakis, 'God First and Last: Religious Traditions and Music of the Yaresan of Guran' (2020)
